meaningful to the growth rate of
corporate profits and equity valuations.
So, I mean, I guess productivity has to
be a story because it doesn't seem like
we're getting any population growth or
growth in the workforce. So,
can productivity bridge that gap to
economic growth for the US economy?
Yeah, I think it already has. I mean,
look, you're talking about a working age
population growth rate of about 50 basis
points. So, that's not much for GDP, uh
but when you look at the actual growth
of the economy, we've been growing at or
above trend for years now. Uh and a lot
of that's come from productivity growth.
Uh if you think about the peak of the
core parts of the labor market that are
most impacted by AI, so you take private
services, X out healthcare, X out
government, X out social services, that
statistic peaked 2 years ago. Mhm.
2 years ago.

even with some black swans like the war
in Iran
um and spiking energy prices,
this economy seems to be chugging along
pretty well here. Absolutely. Yeah,
again, productivity is the number one
thing you got to think about with the
economy, but the capital goods cycle is
is very impressive. You think about the
things that lead productivity like
equipment investment growing at about 16
17% quarter over quarter annualized, R&D
investment growing at about 7% quarter
over quarter annualized, software
investment growing at about 21% quarter
over quarter annualized. These numbers
are well above trend and they're likely
to continue just looking at the AI CapEx
cycle that we continue to highlight. Do
you see an exuberance out there? I mean,
I don't have the VIX at 12 or 11. I
mean,
we asked earlier of the legendary

Yale, your Yale, is the exuberance
there? We're getting there, but not
quite. Uh our position model tracks 10
different indicators from a long-term
time series perspective and it suggests
that we're about in the 80th percentile.
>> Give us an example of one of those
indicators. Yeah, so
equity implied volatility correlation is
in the third percentile, which implies
that, you know, market neutral hedge
fund exposure is in the 97th percentile.

80th percentile, so there's some room to
run. And there should be room to run

>> So, Darius, what do you think the
Federal Reserve is going to do? We've
had a We had a pretty busy little
meeting last time. We've got a new Fed
chairman coming along. I mean,
as a strategist, is that is that a level
of uncertainty that concerns you or do
you feel like, "Ah, the whole process is
going to work?" Yeah, I think the the
best thing Kevin Warsh can do is to
convince his colleagues to do absolutely
nothing, which I think they're moving in
that direction.
>> Uh if you think about what's happening
from an inflation impulse standpoint,
very wicked inflation impulses. We have
credit growth accelerating. We have a
fiscal impulse right now. So, they would
be loath to to to ease monetary policy
here. However, on the other side of the
mandate, we're talking about again true
private services payrolls having peaked
2 years ago. We're talking about an
upsurge in productivity growth that
could shave off some inflation trend
inflation. So, you think the net result
of this is that they probably should be
doing a whole lot of nothing.
>> Okay, you just mentioned the fiscal
limitations where there's almost
crowding out. We just had the 30-year
bond out printing 5.02 to 5.03%.
Are we at that tipping point where it's
almost old school, almost Darius frankly
before your time, where yield matters,
fiscal policy matters? Uh I I think it's
I don't know that it's going to matter
in a in a on off switch perspective.
However, we've said this for years now.
The number one factor in all of the
global financial markets, in our
opinion, is the fact that there is a
supply demand imbalance in the long end
of the Treasury curve. It's caused you
know a a shift to dovishness in
financing policy out of the Treasury.
It's caused a shift to uh perpetual
expansion of the balance sheet by the
Federal Reserve. And ultimately, it's
going to cause a pretty meaningful uh
rebalancing of the US's net
international investment deficit over
time as the rest of the world sort of
seeks to plug their own capital holes.
So, I mean,
I've been doing this for 40-plus years.
The deficit always has been an issue,
but it's never been an issue. How do you
How do you think about this?
>> Yeah, I think it's And so, we don't
think about it from the perspective of
the the actual budget balance dynamics.
We think about it from the perspective
of the the supply demand for the
securities. If you take the marketable
Treasury debt that's maturing in the
next year plus our annualized fiscal
budget deficit plus the rate of
divestment from the Fed's Treasury
portfolio, that sums north of about $12
trillion up from about $5 trillion, but
that doesn't matter. What matters is its
ratio to global savings. It's about 40%
now as opposed to 20% the long-run mean.
